architecture.summary: CX-Mind is a multimodal large language model for chest X-ray diagnosis that interleaves visual encoding with stepwise reasoning via a "think-answer" decoding pattern. The system encodes CXR images and generates structured reasoning steps followed by diagnostic answers, trained end-to-end using curriculum-guided reinforcement learning with verifiable process rewards. Information flows from image → multimodal encoder → token sequence with embedded reasoning checkpoints → policy-optimized decoding under Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), with rule-based conditional rewards supervising intermediate reasoning quality without a separate learned reward model.
trained_components: GRPO policy parameters for interleaved reasoning decoding

biblio.title: CX-Mind: a pioneering multimodal large language model for interleaved reasoning in chest X-ray via curriculum-guided reinforcement learning

claims.statement: CX-Mind achieves an average performance improvement of 25.1% over comparable CXR-specific models
On a real-world clinical dataset (Rui-CXR), CX-Mind achieves a mean recall@1 across 14 diseases that substantially surpasses the second-best results
CX-Mind significantly outperforms existing medical and general-domain MLLMs in visual understanding, text generation, and spatiotemporal alignment
Multi-center expert evaluations confirm clinical utility across multiple dimensions
Generated 42,828 high-quality interleaved reasoning data points supervised by clinical reports
Constructed instruction-tuning dataset CX-Set comprising 708,473 images and 2,619,148 samples

Existing multimodal models predominantly rely on 'one-time' diagnostic approaches, lacking verifiable supervision of the reasoning process, leading to lengthy reasoning, sparse rewards, and frequent hallucinations

Optimization conducted in two stages under GRPO framework: initially stabilizing basic reasoning with closed-domain tasks, followed by transfer to open-domain diagnostics, incorporating rule-based conditional process rewards to bypass the need for pretrained reward models
